Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - TWO INSTANCES OF INTERPRETABLE NEURAL NET WORK FOR UNIVERSAL APPROXIMATIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

This paper proposes two bottom-up interpretable neural network (NN) constructions for universal approximation, namely Triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). The notable properties are (1) resistance to catastrophic forgetting (2) existence of proof for arbitrarily high accuracies on training dataset (3) for an input _x_ , users can identify specific samples of training data whose activation “fingerprints” are similar to that of _x_ ’s activations. Users can also identify samples that are out of distribution.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Artificial neural networks (NN) have recently seen successful applications in many fields. Modern deep neural network (DNN) architecture, usually trained through the backpropagation mechanism, has been called a black-box because of its lack of interpretability. To tackle this issue, various studies have been performed to understand how a NN works; see the following surveys Arrieta et al. (2020); Gilpin et al. (2018); Tjoa & Guan (2020); Wiegreffe & Marasovi´c (2021). This paper primarily proposes two interpretable models, namely triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). Both possess the following three notable properties: (1) Resistance to _catastrophic forgetting_ . (2) Mathematical proofs for arbitrarily high accuracy on training datasets; experimentally demonstrable with python code and simple common datasets (see supp. materials). (3) Detection of out-of-distribution samples through weak activations.
**Concept disambiguation** . Several concepts have multiple possible definitions. We clarify the definitions used in this paper.
1. Interpretability. We consider only fine-grained interpretation, i.e. we look at the meaning of each single neuron or its activation in our models.

2. Universal approximation. Readers might be familiar with universal approximation of functions on certain conditions, e.g. compact sets. Our models can be more general, e.g. user can freely choose the interpolation function between 2 activations based on knowledge of the local manifold. The function can even be pathological. See appendix A.2.1.

3. Catastrophic forgetting: the tendency for knowledge of previously learned dataset to be abruptly lost as information relevant to a new dataset is incorporated. This definition is a slightly nuanced version of Kirkpatrick et al. (2017). Hence, our models’ resistance to catastrophic forgetting is the following. Given a training dataset _D_ accurately modeled by an architecture _M_ , a new dataset _D[′]_ (especially new, out of distribution dataset) can be incorporated into _M_ without losing accuracy on _D_ . See appendix A.2.2 (analogy to biological system included).

**Related works and interpretability issues** . Recent remarkable studies on universal approximators include the Deep Narrow Network by Kidger & Lyons (2020), DeepONet universal approximation for operators by Lu et al. (2021) and the Broad Learning System by Chen et al. (2019); Hanin (2019); Park et al. (2021); Johnson (2019). While insightful, they do not directly address the eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) issue, especially the blackbox property of the DNN. Similarly, a number of classical papers provide theoretical insights for NN as universal approximators, but interpretability, transparency and fairness issues are not their main focus. The universal approximation theorem by Cybenko (1989) asserts that a NN with a single hidden layer can approximate any function to arbitrarily small error under common conditions, proven by asserting the density of that set of NN

in the function space using classic mathematical theorems. In particular, its theorem 1 uses an abstract proof by contradiction. From the proof, it is not easy to observe the internal mechanism of a NN in a straight-forward manner; consequently modern works that depend on it (e.g. Deep Narrow Network) might inherit the blackbox property. Bottom-up constructions for function approximation using NN then emerged, though they also lack the interpretability (see appendix A.3 for more related works). Also consider a demonstration in Nielsen (2015) that could help improve our understanding of universal approximation.

**Outline** . This paper is arranged as the following. Section 2 shows explicit TNN construction, related results, including _a pencil-and-paper example_ for pedagogical purpose. Likewise, section 3 shows SQANN construction, statements regarding SQANN, _another pencil-and-paper example_ , then experimental results of its application, before we conclude the paper with limitations and future works. Python codes and clearer figures are fully available in supp. materials (also see appendix).

## 2 TRIANGULARLY-CONSTRUCTED NN (TNN)

TNN is the prototype NN for our interpretable universal approximator. SQANN (next section) partially borrows the concept from TNN which will be useful as an easy and manageable illustration to deliver the following ideas: (1) organized activations of neurons and (2) the retrieval of _α_ values as the outputs. The model is _TNN_ ( _x_ ) = _α[T] σ_ ( _Wx_ + _b_ ) where _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], _α, b ∈_ R _[N]_ and _W ∈_ R _[N]_ , where we use sigmoid function _σ_ for simplicity. We start with a simple scalar function _y_ = _f_ ( _x_ ) _∈_ R for _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], thus TNN’s interpretability can be illustrated very clearly.

**Assumption: Linear Ordering** . The interpretability comes from the linear ordering property where higher value of _x_ ( _≈_ 1) will activate more neurons while lower values will activate less neurons as shown in fig 1(A). Then _α_ values will be retrieved in a continuous way through dot product, eventually used to compute the output for prediction. In time series, such as ECG (Electrocardiogram), signals can be approximated point-wise (although it is still preferable to have a noise model during preprocessing to prevent overfitting the noise). Meaningful interpretation can be given, for example, by mapping PQRST segments from ECG to specific neurons within TNN, giving some neurons specific meaning and thus interpretability. For more remarks and definition of formal _linear ordering_ etc, see appendix A.4.

We proceed with SQANN, a universal approximation inspired by TNN that allows multi-dimensional input, multi-layer stacking of neurons based on relative strength of neuron activations.

## 3 SEMI-QUANTIZED ACTIVATION NEURAL NETWORK (SQANN)

SQANN architecture is a multi-dimensional universal approximator which (1) retains TNN’s idea of using an organized sequence of activations to retrieve _α_ , (2) remotely resembles a Radial Basis Function, but (3) has deep neural network properties, such as the possibility of deep learning (multiple constructed layers) and neuron activations. The difference is, a neuron in SQANN corresponds exactly to a data sample as SQANN stores its “fingerprints” as neurons’ nuclei as shown in fig. 3(B). The neurons can be activated with different kinds of responses: (1) distinct peaks and (2) half-activations and (3) weak/zero activations, made possible by double selective activation _σdsa_ . They are illustrated in fig. 3(A) and the sketch of proof of proposition 3 in appendix A.5.4, _intuition behind σdsa_ .

The aforementioned distinct types of activation are key to SQANN’s main result and give the model an interpretability at least in the following sense: samples are highly/moderately/not recognizable if their activation patterns strongly/moderately/weakly resemble the activation patterns of an existing training sample _x_ , where identifications are facilitated by the distinct regions of _σdsa_ . The design is “semi” quantized since _σdsa_ has approximately “distinct” levels yet remains continuous. Since SQANN incorporates multi-layer structure, it avoids being a non-generalizing model that nearest neighbours methods suffer from; see the remark in (Pedregosa et al. (2011a)).

## 3.1 SQANN CONSTRUCTION

**Outline of SQANN construction with interpretations** . SQANN is constructed without optimization like gradient descent. Each indexed training data sample is converted into a “fingerprint” or pattern of neuron activations, which undergoes one of the following:

1. _Admission to layer k_ . Sample’s new/distinct fingerprint is added into layer _k_ if the sample weakly activates existing nodes in the layer ( _∀j,_ [ _vk_ ] _j < τad_ ) and no collision occurs; see fig. 3(D.1).

2. _Collision_ . A sample activates one or more neurons strongly i.e. _∃j, k,_ [ _vk_ ] _j > τact_ . The earliest layer where collision occurs is denoted _lc_ . Such sample is integrated into _lc_ , thus very similar samples are _selectively clustered_ . See fig. 3(D.2); the concept is also illustrated in the sketch of proof for proposition 3, appendix A.5.4.

3. _Filtering into deeper layer_ occurs when neither of the above occurs (no strong activation, some moderate activations). Such sample has features loosely similar to previously seen samples, but we need to filter them further to distinguish its finer features.

At this point, it may be clearer to readers how SQANN is constructed. In short, for each layer, _representative activations_ become the neurons of the layer. In layer 1, representatives activations are the samples themselves. In deeper layers, they are activations propagated to the layer.

Initialize by first checking _vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ , the activation of _u[<]_[1] _[>]_ at layer _k_ for collision; if collision occurs, see next paragraph, otherwise, set _Nk_ = ( _ηk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) _, αk_ = ( _yk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) i.e. _ηk[<]_[1] _[>] ← vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ . Similar to layer 1 construction, perform _check Nk activation_ on ( _u[<i>] , y[<i>]_ ) for _i >_ 1 by computing activation _vk[<i>]_ and checking it against the existing nodes. One of the three cases occur (1) admission, when [ _vk[<i>]_ ] _j < τad_ for all _j_ = 1 _, . . . , nk_ and no collision (2) collision, when there exists index _j_ at a _collided layer lc ≤ k_ such that [ _vl[<i>] c_ ] _j > τact_ or (3) otherwise. If (1) occurs, the activation _vk[<i>]_ is added as a new neuron to the layer, _Nk → concat_ ( _Nk, vk[<i>]_ ). If (3) occurs, filter the data sample for deeper layer. Assuming no collision, the process is repeated for the next unused data sample _u[<i>]_ until all remaining data samples are checked. Once done, repeat the process for layer _k_ + 1 construction.

Suppose collision happens when we check _u[<m>]_ at layer _lc_ , we use the collision resolution mechanism. We destroy all layers _l > lc_ and put the collided sample into _lc_ , i.e. _Nlc → concat_ ( _Nlc , vk[<m>]_ ) and _αlc → concat_ ( _αlc , yk[<m>]_ ) (push-node in the pseudo code). No layer will be destroyed if _lc_ = _k_ , the current layer. The data samples used in each destroyed layer are returned to the list of unused samples in the same order they have been used during the construction (returnindex() in the pseudo code); we refer to this as _order integrity_ . Once the colliding sample is added to _lc_ , effectively, the strong activation in this layer is now overshadowed by maximum activation ( _selective clustering_ in action), since the exact neuron is now included as the representative of itself and its locality. This is possibly a practically inefficient process, since we tear down intermediate layers, but we only prioritize the completion of the construction for now.

Catastrophic forgetting resistance is proven similarly: when new samples are used for training, previous samples are not forgotten since SQANN stores the particular fingerprint _ηl[<k>]_ for each sample.

## 3.2 EXPERIMENT TO TEST GENERALIZABILITY OF SQANN

**Test datasets with increasing spread from training distribution** . The accuracy of SQANN on high-dimensional dataset outside the training dataset is harder to formalize in theorems. Furthermore, real life data is often noisy and possibly not regularly structured. We avoid making any related statements for SQANN for now. We instead provide empirical results on test datasets that are _similar_ to the training dataset, to the extent that each point in the test dataset is a training sample perturbed by uniform random values of increasing magnitude. We refer to the noise magnitude as the _test data spread_ . Fig. 4(A,A.2) show four domains _X_ with different test data spread. Test dataset that has larger test data spread contains data samples that are noisier and further away from the training data points. Fig. 4(B,B.2) show that SQANN naturally performs better with smaller test data spread. As the test data spread increases, larger errors are observed. Likewise, smaller spread means smaller _Ninterp_ , i.e. fewer data samples fail to activate neurons in SQANN strongly. For all, training errors are 0 _as expected from theorem 2_ .

**Classification and visualization of SQANN’s special interpretability features** . We show the use of SQANN for a simple classification problem in fig. 3(C). The ring outside is labelled 0 _._ 5, while the ring inside 1 _._ 0. With activation parameters _a_ 1 = _a_ 2 = 1, we achieve zero error not only for training dataset (to be expected from theorem 2) but also on test dataset. A special feature in SQANN is its ability to tell the user which data samples fail to activate any neurons strongly; such samples’ output must be interpolated (see SQANN propagation). In fig. 3(C) right, points marked with red open circles need interpolation. Each such point is interpolated using two training samples whose “fingerprint” neurons are most strongly excited. These two samples are shown as the two

**SQANN is tested for regression on Boston Housing and Diabetes Datasets** to demonstrate its generalizability to unseen/test samples, as the following (simplified here): (1) A small subset of samples _D ⊆D_ (the first 20% of full dataset _D_ ) is used to train SQANN and 9 other regression methods. (2) Mean Squared Errors (MSE) values are measured on unused data _Dtest_ on all 10 models; we expect large errors on some test samples. (3) SQANN’s activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors _eτ_ ( _x_ ) = _|SQANN_ ( _x_ ) _− y_ 0 _| > τ_ and we treat them as out-ofdistribution (OOD) samples. These samples are considered as new distinct samples to be integrated into _D_ as the new training dataset _D[′]_ . (4) Train the 10 models, now with _D[′]_ . ## 4 CONCLUSION, LIMITATION AND FUTURE DIRECTION

**Limitations and future directions** . **TNN** is clearly limited in regards to its application to multidimensional input data although it can be useful on different types of time series data. **SQANN** limitation and possible future development currently include 1) simple sequential drawing of samples that may result in the imbalance of layer size. In the future, more sophisticated ordering of training samples can be used so that layers are constructed with meaningful and purposeful arrangement e.g. deeper layers can be purposefully reserved for rare cases; more research on this is necessary to optimize the results 2) layer destruction during the treatment of collision cases might be an inefficient mechanism, which can be improved in the future.

**Conclusion** , we have proposed TNN and SQANN, two interpretable NNs for universal approximation designed to (1) be resistant to catastrophic forgetting (2) have provably high accuracy on training datasets and (3) can be used to handle out-of-distribution samples.

This paper introduces function approximators with novel properties. It is purely mathematical and algorithmic. No specific ethical issues are present. The ethical context depends only on the dataset but in this paper, only common public datasets have been used.

All codes are available in the supp. materials (to be released to public repository in case of acceptance). Results are easily reproducible even with without random number seeding since data distributions are sufficiently controlled. Jupyter notebook for our particular results are also present. Proofs are all included in the appendix, with sketch of proof and additional statement of assumptions where applicable.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel, bottom-up approach to constructing neural networks, focusing on architectural transparency and resistance to catastrophic forgetting. The proposal of Triangularly-constructed NNs (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NNs (SQANN) is conceptually intriguing, particularly the shift toward "fine-grained" interpretability where specific neurons are mapped to identifiable data features. However, the manuscript requires significant refinement to meet the rigor expected of a top-tier venue like ICLR. While the authors outline ambitious claims—such as resistance to catastrophic forgetting and universal approximation proofs—the current presentation relies heavily on pedagogical "pencil-and-paper" examples and lacks a robust empirical evaluation against state-of-the-art baselines. Furthermore, the reliance on an assumption of "linear ordering" for input data represents a substantial constraint that may limit the model's generalizability to high-dimensional or non-sequential datasets. To improve the paper, the authors must clarify how these models scale beyond toy problems, justify their definition of interpretability in the context of complex manifolds, and provide more rigorous mathematical analysis to substantiate their claims of "arbitrarily high accuracy" compared to standard backpropagation-trained networks.

comments.title: Index indexing and definition ambiguity in TNN weight derivation
comments.quote: We can rewrite the indices so that $W_k = 2a/\Delta_{(N-k+1)}$ and $b_k = a - W_k x_{(N-k+1)}$
comments.explanation: The derivation of weight indices and the boundary condition for $\Delta_{(N)}$ is brittle and potentially circular. The reliance on an undefined dummy variable $x_{(N+1)}$ and the inconsistent index remapping ($N-k+1$) make the construction non-reproducible as written. These notationally confusing steps obscure the algorithmic logic, which is critical for ensuring the proposed TNN architecture is well-defined.

comments.title: Inconsistent and ambiguous indexing in SQANN architecture
comments.quote: [v_{k+1}]_j = \sigma_{dsa}(||v_k - \eta_k[<j>]||)
comments.explanation: The notation for layer activation and fingerprint indexing is used inconsistently throughout the paper. Specifically, the subscripts ($k$ vs $k+1$) frequently mismatch when referencing the layer-to-layer mapping and collision triggers. The use of 'c' versus 'lc' in collision conditions is also confusing and prevents a clear implementation of the recursive node activation logic.

comments.title: Lack of definition for collision resolution and similarity metrics
comments.quote: New sample causing $[v_1^{(k)}]_j = 1$ collision is unresolvable
comments.explanation: The paper relies on 'collision resolution' and 'selective clustering' but fails to define a mathematical criterion for when samples are 'similar' or how to handle 'unresolvable' collisions (where activations hit 1). For a method claiming to provide universal approximation and perfect accuracy, these gaps in handling boundary conditions significantly undermine the algorithm's robustness.

comments.title: Underspecified activation function parameters
comments.explanation: The threshold activation function $sg(x, a)$ introduces a parameter $r$ that is not utilized in the provided formula. Without a definition of how $r$ interacts with the other variables, the exact functional form of the activation is impossible to implement consistently.

comments.title: Mathematical errors in interpolation and pseudocode
comments.quote: y = V_ 1[ αmV_ ]1 i ++ VV_ 22[ αn_ ] j
comments.explanation: The interpolation formula and the destruction loop in the pseudocode contain garbled syntax and/or illogical indexing. Specifically, the interpolation expression is mathematically incoherent, and the pseudocode's loop range for layer destruction creates off-by-one ambiguity.

comments.title: Logical gap in mid-point interpolation claim
comments.quote: The mid-point $x_{mid,k} = 1/2(x_{(k)} + x_{(k+1)})$ takes the value of $\alpha^T \sigma(Wx_{mid,k} + b) = 1/2(y_{(k)} + y_{(k+1)})$
comments.explanation: The authors claim an exact linear identity for a non-linear network structure with sigmoid activations. While they concede it is an 'ideal condition,' it is presented as a property of the construction, which is mathematically invalid for general sigmoidal NNs. This obscures the actual accuracy limitations of the model.

comments.title: Use of technically misleading notation for NN components
comments.quote: The model is $TNN(x) = \alpha^T \sigma(Wx + b)$ where $x \in [0, 1]$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$
comments.explanation: Defining a weight vector $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$ mixed with scalar inputs $x$ using the notation $Wx$ is non-standard and leads to potential ambiguity regarding dimension matching. While the intent is clear to experienced practitioners, it adds unnecessary friction to the reader's understanding of the model's structure.

However, the manuscript requires significant refinement to meet the rigor expected of a top-tier venue like ICLR. While the authors outline ambitious claims—such as resistance to catastrophic forgetting and universal approximation proofs—the current presentation relies heavily on pedagogical "pencil-and-paper" examples and lacks a robust empirical evaluation against state-of-the-art baselines. Furthermore, the reliance on an assumption of "linear ordering" for input data represents a substantial constraint that may limit the model's generalizability to high-dimensional or non-sequential datasets. To improve the paper, the authors must clarify how these models scale beyond toy problems, justify their definition of interpretability in the context of complex manifolds, and provide more rigorous mathematical analysis to substantiate their claims of "arbitrarily high accuracy" compared to standard backpropagation-trained networks.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in TNN weight definition
comments.quote: We can rewrite the indices so that $W_k = 2a/\Delta_{(N-k+1)}$ and $b_k = a - W_k x_{(N-k+1)}$ whenever convenient.
comments.explanation: The authors define TNN such that $x_{(N)}$ is the smallest input (activating 1 neuron) and $x_{(1)}$ is the largest (activating all neurons). Throughout the derivation, the indices align with this ordering. However, in the provided index remapping, using $N-k+1$ for $W_k$ and $b_k$ is confusing. If $k=1$ (the neuron intended to be activated by the smallest input $x_{(N)}$), the formula yields $W_1 = 2a/\Delta_{(N)}$, which is correct. But the definition of $\Delta_{(k)}$ earlier was defined as $x_{(k)} - x_{(k+1)}$. For $k=N$, $\Delta_{(N)}$ involves $x_{(N)} - x_{(N+1)}$, where $x_{(N+1)}$ is the 'dummy' value. While the authors discuss the dummy value, the index logic here is brittle and likely to cause implementation errors for a reader attempting to code this, as the mapping between index $k$ of the weight vector and the specific sample $x_{(k)}$ is shifted by $N$.

comments.title: Dimensionality mismatch in TNN model definition
comments.quote: The model is $TNN(x) = \alpha^T \sigma(Wx + b)$ where $x \in [0, 1]$, $\alpha, b \in \mathbb{R}^N$ and $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$
comments.explanation: The text defines $x$ as a scalar ($x \in [0, 1]$), which aligns with the subsequent usage in the 'Linear Ordering' assumption. However, it defines $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}^N$. In the expression $Wx+b$, if $W$ is a vector and $x$ is a scalar, this is operationally $xW + b$. This is mathematically representable, but standard NN notation $Wx$ usually implies a matrix multiplication ($W \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times M}$). Given the later usage ($Wx_{(1)} + b$), it is clear that $W$ is meant to be a vector of weights, but the definition $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$ makes the expression $Wx$ ambiguous. It's a minor notation issue, but it could confuse readers whether $W$ is a scalar or a vector-like parameter set.

comments.title: Unclear definition of the dummy value for Delta
comments.quote: For $\Delta_{(N)}$, we need a 1 dummy $x_{(N+1)}$ value or we can directly choose its value, e.g. $\sum_{k=1}^N -1\Delta_{(k)}$.
comments.explanation: The expression $\sum_{k=1}^N -1\Delta_{(k)}$ is ill-defined as written. $\Delta_{(k)}$ depends on $x_{(k)}$ and $x_{(k+1)}$, making the summation a telescoping sum that relies on the very dummy value $x_{(N+1)}$ it is trying to define. If the intention is to set the boundary condition for the triangular construction at the left end of the interval, this explanation is circular. While the authors state it 'should not pose any problem', the lack of a concrete definition for the boundary condition makes the construction algorithm non-reproducible as stated.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in TNN weight definition
comments.quote: We can rewrite the indices so that $W_k = 2a/\Delta_{(N-k+1)}$ and $b_k = a - W_kx_{(N-k+1)}$ whenever convenient.
comments.explanation: The derivation previously defines $W_{N-k+1}$ and $b_{N-k+1}$ based on $\Delta_{(k)}$. When re-indexing to $W_k$, the subscript of $\Delta$ should logically correspond to the progression of samples. If $W_k$ corresponds to the $k$-th weight, the index of $\Delta$ should typically be $k$ or $N-k+1$ consistently. The provided re-indexing using $N-k+1$ in $\Delta$ appears to shift the alignment between the $j$-th neuron and the $j$-th data interval in a way that is ambiguous for reproducibility without specific clarification on whether the neurons are ordered by input intensity or index magnitude.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in TNN matrix inversion description
comments.quote: $A^{-1}_{ij} = 1$ along the diagonal, $A_{i,i-1} = -1$ and zeroes otherwise
comments.explanation: The text describes $A^{-1}$ but the second condition identifies an element of $A$ ($A_{i,i-1} = -1$). Given that the transition from $y = A\alpha$ to $\alpha = A^{-1}y$ is central to the TNN construction, the mixture of notation between the forward matrix $A$ and its inverse $A^{-1}$ for the same index rule is confusing. While the context implies the structure of the inverse, it is mathematically sloppy.

comments.title: Ambiguity in SQANN collision resolution mechanism
comments.quote: Exclusively for layer 1, sample will simply be admitted into the layer for selective clustering. New sample causing $[v_1^{(k)}]_j = 1$ collision is unresolvable
comments.explanation: The authors state that for layer 1, collision leads to selective clustering, but then state a specific case (where the activation is exactly 1, meaning identical inputs) is 'unresolvable'. This is problematic because in most practical datasets, duplicate inputs (especially in multi-dimensional feature spaces) are common. The text does not specify how the model handles duplicate data points during training, which is a critical boundary condition for a 'perfect accuracy' model.

comments.title: Under-specified threshold function in SQANN
comments.explanation: The formula includes a parameter $r$ (likely related to the 'selectivity' or width of the activation region), but $r$ does not appear in the provided expression for $sg(x, a)$. If $r$ is a scaling factor for $x$ or $a$, or if it relates to the 'double' nature of the activation, it is not explicitly used in the functional form provided. This makes the $\sigma_{dsa}$ calculation impossible to reproduce exactly.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Node Activation Definition
comments.quote: [ v_k+1 ] _j = \sigma_{dsa} \left( ||v_k - \eta_{k+1}^{<j>} || \right)
comments.explanation: In Section 3 (Nodes activation), the formula defines the activation of node j in layer k+1 as a function of the difference between the input v_k and the stored fingerprint. However, the text immediately above this defines eta_{k+1}^{<j>} as an element of N_{k+1}. The notation eta_{k+1}^{<j>} is spatially/index-wise ambiguous: usually, the fingerprint for index j should be associated with the index relative to that specific layer (N_{k+1}). The inconsistency lies in the subscript usage: does the index k+1 refer to the layer being computed into, or is it an error for eta_k? Given that the preceding text refers to 'Layer k' and 'Layer k+1' as distinct, the indexing is confusingly mixed throughout the paragraph.

comments.title: Inconsistent Threshold Logic for Admission
comments.quote: If for all j such that [ v_1^{(2)} ] _j < \tau_{ad} , then N_1 \rightarrow concat( N_1, x^{(k)} )
comments.explanation: In the construction of Layer 1, the condition for admission is listed as '[v_1^{(2)}]_j < \tau_{ad}'. The superscript '(2)' appears to be a typo for the current sample index '(k)', as the pseudocode logic immediately defines k > 2. While the intent is clear (admit if activation is below threshold), the use of the specific index (2) in place of the generic index (k) is a technical error that makes the algorithmic step appear to depend on a hardcoded second sample rather than the running sample.

comments.title: Underspecified Collision Handling
comments.quote: New sample causing [ v_1^{(k)} ] _j = 1 collision is unresolvable; see appendix
comments.explanation: The text states that collision where activation equals exactly 1 is 'unresolvable', yet it does not specify what the system should do in this case (e.g., skip, error, or re-initialize). For a methodology paper claiming universal approximation properties, leaving the handling of an 'unresolvable' collision undefined without explicitly stating the implication for the guarantee of accuracy is a significant gap in the robustness of the construction.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Selective Clustering
comments.quote: if y_1, y_2 are similar, then p_1, p_2 are clustered together, otherwise two distinct clusters are created
comments.explanation: The criterion for 'similarly' is not defined. In an academic context, 'similar' for y1 and y2 requires a metric or threshold (like the $\tau_{act}$ mentioned later). Since this term is used to justify why the model avoids the generalizability issues of nearest-neighbor methods, the lack of a formal definition for 'similar' prevents a rigorous assessment of whether this clustering actually solves the mentioned non-generalization problem or merely masks it.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in interpolation formula
comments.quote: y = V_ 1[ αmV_ ]1 i ++ VV_ 22[ αn_ ] j
comments.explanation: The interpolation formula appears to have been garbled by OCR or a typo. It contains redundant/conflicting terms like 'VV_ 22' and 'i ++', and the subscripting/indexing '[ αmV_ ]' is unconventional and inconsistent with the previously defined notation ('α_k[<j>]'). While the intent—a weighted sum or linear interpolation between the two most activated neurons—is clear from the surrounding text, the formal representation is mathematically nonsensical and prevents reproduction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'collision' condition indices
comments.quote: [ v_l[<i>] c ] _j > τact
comments.explanation: In the SQANN construction section, the index 'c' appears as '[ v_l[<i>] c ]'. The context implies this refers to the activation at the layer where the collision occurred, previously denoted 'lc'. If 'c' is meant to be 'lc', the subscripting is confusing, as 'l_c' suggests a layer variable 'l' with subscript 'c'. If 'c' is an independent variable, it is undefined. Given that the text defines 'lc' as the layer of collision, this is likely a notation error that obscures the definition of the collision trigger.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of SQANN-propagated activation input
comments.quote: [ vk ] _j = σdsa ( || vk− 1 − ηk[<j>] || )
comments.explanation: The node activation formula in the 'Computing output' section uses '|| vk−1 - ηk[<j>] ||'. However, in the 'Nodes activation' section (Section 3), it was defined as '|| vk - ηk[<j>] ||' (where the input is the output of the *previous* layer). While 'vk-1' is the logical intended input for layer 'k', there is a lack of indexing clarity: is the input to layer 'k' always the output of 'k-1', or can it be any layer 'k'? This creates ambiguity regarding the recursive structure of the network and whether propagation is strictly sequential.

comments.title: Index indexing confusion in Pseudo code 1
comments.quote: for $l_j$ from $l_c+1$ to $l_{now}+1:
comments.explanation: In the 'collision' case within the pseudocode, the logic for destroying and re-processing layers is described as iterating 'from $l_c+1$ to $l_{now}+1$'. In the summary context (Collision Resolution), it states: 'If a collision occurs at layer $l_c$, layers $l > l_c$ are destroyed'. The pseudocode's loop range suggests destroying layers up to $l_{now}$, but the index $l_c+1$ to $l_{now}+1$ creates an off-by-one ambiguity regarding whether the current layer ($l_{now}$) or the layer immediately deeper is cleared. Furthermore, 'return index(l_j)' inside the loop is syntactically and logically confusing without a clear return-to-list action.

comments.title: Inconsistency in SQANN node activation indexing
comments.quote: [v_{k+1}]_j = \sigma_{dsa}(||v_k - \eta_k[<j>]||)
comments.explanation: According to the definition, $v_k$ is the activation of layer $k$. The equation for $[v_{k+1}]_j$ uses $\eta_k[<j>]$. However, $\eta_k$ typically denotes the 'synapse' fingerprints stored in layer $k$. The equation implies that the activation of the *next* layer depends on comparing the activation of the *current* layer to the fingerprints of the *current* layer. This appears to be a mismatch; usually, a layer's weight/fingerprint matrix would be indexed by the destination layer's parameters ($N_{k+1}$). If $\eta_k$ is meant to represent synaptic connections to layer $k+1$, it is notationally inconsistent with the previous definition where $N_k$ contains $\eta_k$.

comments.title: Unclear handling of 'unresolvable' layer 1 collisions
comments.quote: New sample causing $[v_1[(k)]]_j = 1$ collision is unresolvable; see appendix on ill-defined datasets
comments.explanation: The text states that a collision resulting in an activation of exactly 1 is 'unresolvable'. However, in a real-world implementation, numerical precision (floating point equality) makes a result of *exactly* 1 rare. The paper does not specify how the system behaves if a value hits this singularity, beyond pointing to an appendix. As this is presented as a fundamental property of the SQANN construction, the lack of a defined fallback mechanism (e.g., jittering, or explicit handling) for this 'unresolvable' case creates a gap in the claimed robustness of the algorithm.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Mid-point' interpolation definition
comments.quote: The mid-point $x_{mid,k} = 1/2(x_{(k)} + x_{(k+1)})$ takes the value of $\alpha^T \sigma(Wx_{mid,k} + b) = 1/2(y_{(k)} + y_{(k+1)})$
comments.explanation: Proposition 2 claims an exact equality for the mid-point. Given that the activation function $\sigma$ is nonlinear (sigmoid), the linearity $1/2(f(x_1) + f(x_2)) = f(1/2(x_1 + x_2))$ only holds if the function $f$ is linear. Since the NN is constructed using sigmoids, this property is generally only valid in the limit $a \to \infty$ or under specific constraints. The proposition title claims this is a formal property, but the text admits 'Admittedly, this is an ideal condition', which contradicts the potential implication that it is a general mathematical result.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in collision definition
comments.quote: when there exists index j at a collided layer lc <= k such that [ vl[<j>]c ] j > τact
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the condition for collision at layer l_c uses indices that are confusing and potentially misaligned with the earlier notation. The text says [ vl[<j>]c ] j > τact, but based on the definition of node activation components [vk]_j, the component index should be the j-th component of the activation at layer l_c, not an index inside the activation vector notation itself. Furthermore, it is unclear if the superscript/subscript indices are being handled correctly for the target neuron j in the collided layer. While context suggests a check against the threshold for a specific neuron j, the OCR/notation here ([ vl[<j>]c ] j) is syntactically muddled compared to the established [vk+1]_j notation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'linear ordering' assumptions
comments.quote: for ∆[(N)], we need a 1 dummy x[(N+1)] value or we can directly choose its value, e.g. N Σ k N =1 −1∆[(k)]
comments.explanation: The formula for the dummy value, provided as Σ_{k=1}^N -1∆[(k)], appears to have garbled notation (likely a transcription error from LaTeX). A reader cannot determine the intended value of x[(N+1)]. While the text notes this only affects the 'left end' of the graph, it is an underspecified boundary condition for an algorithm that claims universal approximation.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in Layer 1 activation definition
comments.explanation: In Section 3, node activation is defined as [v_k+1]_j = σ_dsa(||v_k - η_k[<j>]||). However, for Layer 1, the text switches to [v_1]_j = σ_dsa(||x - η_1[<j>]||). While this makes intuitive sense (using input x instead of v_0), it is technically inconsistent with the previous recursive definition style. A reader might be confused as to whether x is treated as a v_0 vector of the same dimension as v_k. Although minor, it impacts the consistency of the 'deep' architecture definition.

comments.title: Potential overclaim regarding 'no optimization'
comments.quote: SQANN is constructed without optimization like gradient descent.
comments.explanation: The authors claim there is no gradient-based optimization; however, the construction relies on thresholds τ_ad and τ_act, and the 'collision resolution' involves layer teardown and re-processing. The efficiency and convergence of this construction are highly dependent on these thresholds. While technically not 'backpropagation,' the process involves hyperparameter tuning that functions similarly to an iterative optimizer. The claim of being a 'bottom-up construction' is valid, but calling it 'without optimization' obscures the reliance on threshold selection, which is a form of parameter optimization for the construction process.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents an ambitious proposal for two interpretable neural network architectures—TNN and SQANN—claiming universal approximation capabilities, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, and fine-grained neuron-level interpretability via activation “fingerprints.” While the motivation to bridge universal approximation with transparent, continuously updatable models is timely and potentially valuable, the current draft suffers from significant clarity and rigor issues that undermine its scientific contribution. The mathematical notation is inconsistent and frequently improperly typeset (e.g., scattered subscripts, undefined vector/matrix dimensions, and broken equations), which obscures the proposed constructions and makes it impossible to verify the promised “proofs” for arbitrarily high training accuracy and catastrophic forgetting resistance. Moreover, the core interpretability claims lack formal definitions and empirical grounding: the concept of “linear ordering” is ambiguously defined, the link between activation patterns and fine-grained neuron semantics is asserted rather than demonstrated, and the assertion that the models can reliably identify similar or out-of-distribution samples is presented without quantitative methodology or baseline comparisons. To advance this work, the authors must replace descriptive claims with rigorous, well-stated theorems, clarify the precise mechanisms behind forgetting resistance and OOD detection, properly position their approach within the established XAI and continual learning literature, and provide reproducible experiments that substantiate all performance statements. Until these foundational issues are resolved, the novelty, correctness, and practical utility of the proposed architectures remain difficult to assess.

comments.title: Dimensionality notation mismatch in dataset definition
comments.explanation: Initially, the dimensionality notation in the linear ordering assumption raised a concern. The authors explicitly state earlier that they 'start with a simple scalar function y = f(x) ∈ ℝ for x ∈ [0, 1],' which implies a 1-dimensional input space. However, the dataset is immediately defined as belonging to _R _[n] ×_ R. The inequality chain _[x]_[(] _[N]_[)] _[<][x]_[(] _[N][−]_[1)] _[<][· · ·][<][x]_[(1)]_ requires a total ordering, which is not generally defined for _ℝ^n_ when n > 1. While this may be a minor notation slip (likely intending n=1 for this section), it creates a logical inconsistency between the stated scalar assumption and the generalized dimension notation. A careful reader might wonder if the ordering applies element-wise, via norms, or via a separate scalar feature, none of which are clarified here.

comments.title: Suspicious negative sign in activation summation formula
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction presents a minus sign before the summation index/weights: _[−]_[1] _[α][i][σ]_[(] _[Wx]_[(2)][+] _[ b]_[)] _[i]_. Given the preceding context where _σ(Wx_[2]_ + b) = [1, 1, ..., 1, 0]^T_, the output y_[2]_ should simply be the dot product of _α_ and this activation vector, i.e., _Σ_{i=1}^{N-1} α_i_. A literal negative sign would invert the predicted output or imply negative weights, which contradicts the standard construction of positive activation contributions in this triangular scheme. While this is highly likely an OCR artifact (misreading a formatting dash or index marker as a minus), if taken as intended mathematical notation, it breaks the algebraic derivation of the output. It should be corrected to avoid misleading readers about the sign convention.

comments.title: Vague conditions and underspecified bound in Proposition 1
comments.quote: there exists A ⊆ D[′] such that, using TNN constructed with D ∪A, for all samples in test dataset_ ( _x[′] , y[′]_ ) _∈ D[′] , sample-wise error e_ = _|y[′] − TNN_ ( _x[′]_ ) _| has an upper bound max_ ( _|y[′] − y_[(] _[k]_[+1)] _|, |y[′] − y_[(] _[k]_[)] _|_ ) _for some k.
comments.explanation: Proposition 1 claims that the sample-wise error is bounded by the vertical distance to the two neighboring training y-values. This implies that the TNN interpolation monotonically stays within the convex hull of adjacent training outputs. While this aligns with the 'triangular' activation concept for large 'a', the proposition's preamble is logically underspecified. It states 'there exists A ⊆ D′ such that...', but does not define how A is chosen, what properties A must satisfy, or how the bound applies when A is added to the training set. The phrase reads more like a high-level heuristic guarantee than a rigorous theorem. A careful reader would need to know how the augmented dataset D ∪ A affects the weight matrix W and b, and whether the bound holds for all x′ or only those falling within specific intervals. Clarifying the construction of A and the exact domain of applicability would resolve this ambiguity.

comments.title: Notation collision: Symbol 'A' used for both OOD sample set and triangular matrix
comments.quote: Otherwise, there are out-of-distribution (ood) samples A ⊆ D′ , which can be incorporated into the training dataset. ... we get y = Aα where A is an upper-left triangular matrix
comments.explanation: The symbol 'A' is used to denote the subset of out-of-distribution test samples (A ⊆ D′) in the paragraph preceding Proposition 1. Shortly after, in the derivation of α, 'A' is used to denote the triangular matrix mapping α to y (and its inverse A^{-1}). This notation collision creates significant ambiguity, especially since the matrix A is inverted and discussed in detail. A careful reader could easily conflate the set A with the matrix A, or struggle to track which refers to which across the section. The authors should use distinct symbols (e.g., D_ood and M or T).

comments.title: Figure 2 caption lists undefined parameters
comments.quote: The parameters are as the following A = 1 , λ = − 1 , C = 0 for all, (B2) A 1 = 0 . 1 , B 1 = 20 , C 1 = 1 (B3) A 1 = 0 . 1 , B 1 = 10 , C 1 = 1.
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 2 lists parameters A, λ, C, and indexed variants like A_1, B_1, C_1. However, the TNN model defined in Section 2 strictly uses parameters α, W, b, and the sharpness scalar a. The symbols A, λ, C do not appear in the TNN formulation or any preceding text. This strongly suggests a copy-paste error from another experiment, model, or draft, or an incomplete update of the caption. Without clarification, readers cannot verify or reproduce the specific plots in Figure 2.

comments.title: Ambiguous/underspecified formula for dummy Δ^(N)
comments.quote: For ∆[(] _[N]_[)] , we need a 1 dummy x[(] _[N]_[+1)] value or we can directly choose its value, e.g. _N_[Σ] _k[N]_ =1 _[−]_[1][∆][(] _[k]_[)][.
comments.explanation: The text discusses choosing a boundary value for Δ^(N) to handle the left end of the graph near x=0. The proposed example value is garbled by notation/OCR: `_N_[Σ] _k[N]_ =1 _[−]_[1][∆]`. This likely attempts to express a telescoping sum like $-\sum_{k=1}^{N-1} \Delta^{(k)}$ or similar, but the notation is unclear and syntactically broken. Given that the boundary condition directly affects the shape of the approximation at x=0 (as noted in the text), leaving this underspecified or ambiguous hinders reproducibility. Readers would need to guess the intended sign and summation limits.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in pencil-and-paper example
comments.explanation: The derived weight formula is $W_{N-k+1} = 2a/\Delta^{(k)}$. For the provided dataset, $\Delta^{(1)} = 0.5$ and $\Delta^{(2)} = 0.5$. If we use the example's slope of 20, then $20 = 2a/0.5 \Rightarrow a = 5$. However, the thresholds in the example ($20x+5$, etc.) correspond to $a=10$ (since $\sigma(\pm 10) \approx 0/1$ and $2a/\Delta = 20$ implies $a=5$ for $\Delta=0.5$, or $a=10$ for $\Delta=1$). The example appears to use $a=10$ but scales the weights as if $\Delta=1$, or simply picked round numbers for pedagogical clarity rather than strict adherence to the derived equations. While acceptable as a loose illustration, it contradicts the formal derivation if taken literally and may confuse readers attempting to verify the numbers.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology 'upper-left triangular matrix'
comments.quote: we get y = Aα where A is an upper-left triangular matrix
comments.explanation: The term 'upper-left triangular' is non-standard in linear algebra. Given the subsequent description of $A^{-1}$ having non-zeros on the diagonal and superdiagonal ($A^{-1}_{i,i}=1, A^{-1}_{i,i+1}=-1$), the matrix A is mathematically an *upper triangular* matrix. The phrasing likely stems from a typographical error or non-native phrasing. While not mathematically fatal, it deviates from standard terminology and could cause momentary confusion for readers checking matrix properties or reproducing the construction.

comments.title: Missing critical equations for σ_dsa and node activation
comments.quote:
then the double selective activation is (fig. 3(A)):

**Nodes activation** . Denote the “synapse” or the activation value of node j at layer k by input v as:
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that mathematical definitions follow, but both the formula for the double selective activation function (σ_dsa) and the node activation rule are completely absent. The passage jumps directly from these introductory phrases to the next section/figure references without providing the equations. Given that σ_dsa governs how similarity is measured and drives the entire admission/collision/filtering logic, omitting its mathematical form fundamentally breaks reproducibility and undermines the model's definitional foundation. Context does not resolve this, as these are core algorithmic components rather than peripheral definitions.

comments.title: Typo in Layer 1 admission condition sample index
comments.quote: If for all j such that [ _v_ 1[(2)][]] _[j][< τ][ad]_[, then] _N_ 1 _→ concat_ ( _N_ 1 _, x_[(] _[k]_[)] ) and _α_ 1 _→ concat_ ( _α_ 1 _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ).
comments.explanation: The algorithm processes training samples sequentially for an arbitrary index k (explicitly stated earlier: 'take a new sample (x_{[k]}, y_{[k]}), k > 2'). However, the admission condition erroneously checks the activation vector indexed at 2 ([v_{1[2]}]_j) instead of the current sample index k ([v_{1[k]}]_j). As written, the code would repeatedly compare new incoming samples against the activation pattern of the second training sample, rather than its own. This appears to be a copy-paste or placeholder typo that would cause the algorithm to fail during actual implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguous index notation in multi-layer forward pass
comments.explanation: The notation η_{k<j>+1} is syntactically and mathematically unclear. Based on the surrounding context, the authors intend to denote the fingerprint of the j-th neuron in the next layer (k+1), which should logically be written as η_{k+1<j>}. The current formatting suggests adding 1 to the relabeled index j, which contradicts the layer-by-layer forwarding description and could lead readers to incorrectly implement the feature mapping. Even allowing for OCR artifacts, the intended mathematical relationship between layer indices and relabeled node indices is not clearly conveyed.

comments.title: Inconsistent layer/sample indexing variables in concatenation description
comments.quote: To denote the addition of the new k-th node to the layer l, use _η_{l< k>_} ← v, where v can be for example _v_{2[m]}_ the activation of the m-th data at layer 2. Alternatively, _N_l_ → concat( _N_l_, _η_{l< k>_}_) ... always correspondingly concatenate the _α_l_ i.e. _y_{l< k>_} ← _y_{[m]}_.
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the subscript k is reserved for the layer index (e.g., Layer k, N_k, α_k), while angle brackets < > denote relabeled sample indices within a layer. Here, the authors switch to layer index l and use k as a node/sample index, directly clashing with the established x_{[k]}, y_{[k]} notation for original dataset samples and η_{k<j>}, y_{k<j>} for relabeled layer data. This variable collision creates unnecessary confusion between original indices, relabeled indices, and layer numbers, potentially leading to misinterpretation during implementation.

comments.title: Muddled threshold-based branching logic
comments.quote: (2) _collision_ occurs, when there exists j such that _τact_ < [ _v_ 1[(] _[k]_[)][]] _[j][<]_[1][,][thus][sample][is][admitted][via][collision][resolution][mechanism.][Exclusively] for layer 1, sample will simply be admitted into the layer for selective clustering. New sample causing [ _v_ 1[(] _[k]_[)][]] _[j]_[= 1][ collision is unresolvable; see appendix on ill-defined datasets (3) or new sample] is _filtered to deeper layers_ , when neither occurs.
comments.explanation: The outline in Section 3.1 clearly establishes three mutually exclusive cases based on activation thresholds: weak (< τ_ad) → admit, strong (> τ_act) → collision (integrated at earliest layer l_c), moderate → filter. However, the detailed Layer 1 construction paragraph blends these conditions. It labels strong activation as 'collision' but immediately follows with 'Exclusively for layer 1, sample will simply be admitted into the layer for selective clustering', blurring the distinction between the collision resolution mechanism and standard admission. Furthermore, it omits the explicit condition for moderate activations in this paragraph, leaving readers without a clear if-else branching structure. While the intent aligns with the outline, the procedural description is logically disjointed and could mislead a reproducer trying to code the decision tree.

comments.title: Undefined variable y_0 in error threshold formula
comments.explanation: The authors correctly propose a standard out-of-distribution (OOD) detection strategy by flagging samples where prediction error exceeds a threshold τ. However, the symbol y_0 appears here to denote the true label but is never defined in the notation section or preceding prose. Throughout the paper, true labels are consistently denoted as y or y^{(k)}. In neural network literature, y_0 conventionally represents a bias term or initial condition, which would make the formula conceptually incorrect. Assuming a typo for y is highly plausible given the context, but as written it introduces an undefined symbol that a careful reader would need to guess.

comments.title: Contradiction between textual claim and Table 1 data for MLP performance
comments.quote: hence we get slightly decreased performance for e2 compared to e5.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that stochastic optimization in MLPs can lead to different local minima when training data composition changes. However, I initially flagged this sentence because it contradicts the numerical results presented in Table 1. The table reports an MSE of 6.121 for the MLP under the e5 experiment and 5.895 under the e2 experiment. Since MSE is a loss metric where lower values indicate better predictive performance, the e2 result actually represents an improvement, not a decrease. The textual claim directly opposes the table's data, which could mislead readers about the model's empirical behavior under the OOD retraining protocol.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of test-set zero error claim
comments.quote: we achieve zero error not only for training dataset (to be expected from theorem 2) but also on test dataset.
comments.explanation: The authors rightly highlight that TNN/SQANN architectures can achieve perfect training fits, which aligns with Theorem 2 and their point-mapped construction. The claim of zero test error in Figure 3(C) is empirically true for that specific, simple 2D ring classification toy example. However, the phrasing loosely couples this empirical observation to Theorem 2 ('but also on test dataset'), which could mislead readers into thinking the architecture theoretically guarantees zero test error. Universal approximation theorems do not ensure zero test error without explicit assumptions about test-point density or distributional match to training manifolds. The claim should be explicitly framed as an empirical result for that specific figure rather than a general property.

comments.title: Self-referential subset notation D ⊆ D
comments.quote: A small subset of samples _D ⊆D_ (the first 20% of full dataset _D_ )
comments.explanation: The authors intend to define a training subset comprising the first 20% of the full dataset. However, they write _D ⊆ D_, which is a mathematically trivial self-subset relation and almost certainly a typographical error. Standard practice would distinguish the subset using a different symbol (e.g., _D_train ⊆ D_ or _S ⊆ D_). While this does not break the methodology, it is a notation inconsistency that adds unnecessary cognitive load for a careful reader checking the experimental setup.
